tier_2_handbook.definition: Peritoneal mesothelioma (PeM) is a rare, diffuse malignant neoplasm originating from the mesothelial cells lining the peritoneal cavity. It represents approximately 15% of all mesotheliomas, with the remainder arising from the pleura (~85%), pericardium, or tunica vaginalis testis (<1%) [1,6-9]. PeM is now classified as a malignant entity by the 2021 World Health Organization (WHO) classification, which removed the term "malignant" from all mesothelioma diagnoses because all mesotheliomas are considered malignant [39,57]. The tumor arises from the serosal lining of the peritoneum and can spread extensively within the abdominal cavity but rarely metastasizes beyond it [24]. Histologically, PeM is subdivided into three main types: epithelioid, biphasic (mixed), and sarcomatoid, each with distinct prognostic and therapeutic implications [17,50]. The diagnosis requires a combination of histologic assessment, immunohistochemistry (IHC) for mesothelial markers (e.g., calretinin, D2-40) and exclusion of carcinoma markers (e.g., claudin-4, TTF-1, polyclonal CEA, PAX8), and occasionally molecular techniques such as fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) or next-generation sequencing (NGS) [23-26]. PeM must be distinguished from benign mesothelial proliferations (e.g., reactive mesothelial hyperplasia, peritoneal inclusion cyst, well-differentiated papillary mesothelial tumor [WDPMT]) and from metastatic carcinomas [24,55]. The mean age at diagnosis is approximately 69 years, and the disease occurs equally in males and females, unlike pleural mesothelioma which predominates in males [11,20]. Patients typically present with abdominal signs and symptoms such as ascites (77%), pain (69%), distension, and abdominal mass (30%), often leading to late diagnosis [11,38]. The incidence of PeM in the United States is estimated at 300–400 cases per year, with 1-year overall survival of about 46% and 5-year overall survival of about 20% [2,10,11,121-124].

subtypes.name: Epithelioid
subtypes.description: Characterized by epithelioid-to-round cells arranged in diverse architectural patterns including tubulopapillary, trabecular, solid, acinar, micropapillary, or adenomatoid. Rare variants include clear cell, signet ring cell, rhabdoid, deciduoid, and small cell subtypes [5-8].
subtypes.key_associations: Better prognosis compared to biphasic or sarcomatoid; median overall survival 39 months (for diffuse PeM) vs. 14 months for biphasic [15]; higher likelihood of BAP1 loss by IHC (50–70% of epithelioid) [31-37]; complete cytoreduction more achievable; primary treatment with CRS + HIPEC is standard for eligible patients [PEM-2, PEM-C].

subtypes.name: Biphasic (mixed)
subtypes.description: Contains both epithelioid and sarcomatoid components, with each comprising at least 10% of the tumor [1].
subtypes.key_associations: Intermediate prognosis between epithelioid and sarcomatoid; median overall survival 14 months [15]; higher risk features; neoadjuvant systemic therapy is strongly encouraged before CRS + HIPEC [PEM-2, PEM-C]; 5-year survival 50.2% with complete resection (CC-0) in selected patients [78].

subtypes.name: Sarcomatoid
subtypes.description: Characterized by spindled cells with tapered nuclei. Subtypes include conventional/spindle cell, desmoplastic, lymphohistiocytoid, and those with heterologous differentiation (osteosarcomatous, chondrosarcomatous, rhabdomyosarcomatous elements) [9-13].
subtypes.key_associations: Worst prognosis; BAP1 loss by IHC occurs in <20% of sarcomatoid [38]; often focal or absent mesothelial marker expression (most sensitive: D2-40/podoplanin) [27]; surgery generally contraindicated; first-line therapy with nivolumab + ipilimumab is preferred [PEM-D 1 of 3].

subtypes.name: Mesothelioma in situ
subtypes.description: New entity in the 2021 WHO classification; a preinvasive, single-layer surface proliferation of neoplastic mesothelial cells diagnosed by loss of BAP1 nuclear expression on IHC and/or CDKN2A homozygous deletion by FISH or MTAP IHC, without mass lesions on imaging or thoracoscopy [1,4].
subtypes.key_associations: Preinvasive stage; requires careful histologic and ancillary testing to distinguish from reactive mesothelial proliferations [22].

subtypes.name: Well-differentiated papillary mesothelial tumor (WDPMT)
subtypes.description: Renamed from well-differentiated papillary mesothelioma in 2021 WHO classification [1]. Often an incidental finding in the peritoneum of those assigned female at birth; characterized by recurrent mutations in TRAF7 or CDC42 [76].
subtypes.key_associations: Indolent behavior; observation for asymptomatic noninvasive disease; CRS ± HIPEC for symptomatic, recurrent, or microinvasive disease [PEM-2, Discussion MS-5].

subtypes.name: Peritoneal inclusion cyst (benign multicystic mesothelioma)
subtypes.description: Benign, rare tumor with multiple mesothelial-lined cysts; previously termed benign multicystic mesothelioma [47-49].
subtypes.key_associations: Benign; distinguished from diffuse PeM by lack of invasion, bland cytology; treatment: observation or surgical excision if symptomatic [PEM-2, Discussion MS-5].

subtypes.name: Adenomatoid tumor
subtypes.description: Benign mesothelial tumor primarily affecting genital tracts; can rarely involve pleura; recurrent mutations in TRAF7 [78].
subtypes.key_associations: Benign behavior; distinct from diffuse PeM; no specific treatment required.

subtypes.name: Molecular subtypes (ALK-rearranged, EWSR1::ATF1 fusion)
subtypes.description: Rare peritoneal mesotheliomas in young adults (≤40 years) harboring ALK rearrangements (e.g., STRN-ALK) or EWSR1::ATF1 fusions [49-51,54,70,71].
subtypes.frequency: Very rare; ALK rearrangements found in ~8% of young patients with PeM [102]
subtypes.key_associations: Dramatic response to ALK inhibitors (e.g., ceritinib, crizotinib) [53,54,104]; distinct from asbestos-associated PeM; broad molecular tumor profiling recommended to identify these driver alterations [PEM-D 1 of 3].

subtypes.name: Localized pleural mesothelioma (note: primarily pleural, but may be included in differential)
subtypes.description: Microscopically identical to diffuse mesothelioma but radiographically and grossly solitary and circumscribed [15-17]. Genetically includes BAP1-mutant, TRAF7-mutant, and near-haploid groups [18].
subtypes.key_associations: Less aggressive clinical course than diffuse mesothelioma; requires radiographic confirmation of solitary lesion [15-18].

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: Peritoneal mesothelioma (PeM) is characterized by recurrent alterations in tumor suppressor genes and epigenetic regulators, with distinct molecular features compared to pleural mesothelioma [40,70]. The most frequently altered gene is BAP1 (BRCA1-associated protein-1), which is inactivated through point mutations, copy number loss, structural rearrangements, and minute chromosomal deletions [62-64,67-69]. BAP1 loss by immunohistochemistry (IHC) is present in 50–70% of epithelioid mesotheliomas but in less than 20% of sarcomatoid type [31-37]. Germline BAP1 mutations define the BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome, a hereditary cancer syndrome associated with mesothelioma, uveal melanoma, cholangiocarcinoma, and clear cell renal cell carcinoma [30,48,72]. Germline mutations are found in 12–16% of patients with pleural and peritoneal mesothelioma, involving genes in DNA repair and cell cycle regulation including BAP1, BRCA2, CDKN2A, TMEM127, VHL, WT1, MRE11A, and MSH6 [48,72,73]. CDKN2A (p16) homozygous deletions are less frequent in PeM (8–26%) compared to pleural mesothelioma (60–74%), as measured by FISH or inferred from MTAP IHC loss (14–16% in PeM) [40-44,57-59]. NF2 hemizygous loss by FISH occurs in ~50% of pleural mesotheliomas but data are less robust for PeM [61]. Other recurrently mutated tumor suppressors include TP53, SETD2, and DDX3X [42,62-66]. Rare genomic near-haploidization with extensive loss of heterozygosity involving nearly all chromosomes except 5 and 7 has been described in pleural mesothelioma, with some cases also showing TP53 and/or SETDB1 mutations [62]. In young adults, oncogenic EWSR1::ATF1 fusions have been identified in pleural and peritoneal mesotheliomas [70,71], and ALK rearrangements (e.g., STRN-ALK) are found in a very rare subset of peritoneal mesothelioma patients, often children and young adults without asbestos exposure or predisposing germline mutations [49-51,54,101-103]. These ALK fusions show dramatic responses to ALK inhibitor therapies such as ceritinib and crizotinib [53,54,104]. Comprehensive genomic profiling is recommended to identify these rare driver alterations [PEM-D 1 of 3].

epidemiology.incidence: In the United States, mesothelioma is estimated to occur in approximately 3,500 people per year [1-4]. Peritoneal mesothelioma (PeM) accounts for about 15% of all mesotheliomas, corresponding to an estimated 300–400 cases per year in the U.S. [6-10]. The incidence of both pleural and peritoneal mesothelioma is decreasing in the U.S. since asbestos use declined in the 1970s, though asbestos continues to be imported [1,5,25-28]. The annual incidence of diffuse pleural mesothelioma is approximately 1 in 100,000 [2,3].
epidemiology.mortality: PeM carries a poor prognosis. One-year overall survival is approximately 46%, and 5-year overall survival is about 20%; cure is rare [11,121-124]. Patients with epithelioid histology have better outcomes than those with biphasic or sarcomatoid histology [15]. Survival is improved for patients who are able to undergo complete cytoreductive surgery (CRS) with intraperitoneal chemotherapy, with median OS of 53 months in a multi-institutional study of CRS + HIPEC (n=401), and 3- and 5-year survival rates of 60% and 47%, respectively [76]. In a large single-institution study (n=108), median OS was 63.2 months, with 19 patients surviving >7 years [77].
epidemiology.trends: Incidence is declining in the U.S. due to reduced asbestos exposure since the 1970s, but global patterns vary; asbestos is still used in many countries [25-28]. PeM incidence may be underreported due to misdiagnosis as ovarian cancer or other peritoneal malignancies [10].
epidemiology.demographics: The mean age at diagnosis is approximately 69 years [11]. PeM is diagnosed in equal numbers of males and females, unlike pleural mesothelioma which is more common in males [11]. PeM may occur in younger patients, including children and young adults, particularly those with germline BAP1 mutations or ALK rearrangements [20,48,102]. Approximately 50% of patients with PeM have positive PD-L1 expression levels [61,85,88].

risk_factors.detail: The primary known risk factor for mesothelioma overall, but PeM is less frequently associated with asbestos than pleural mesothelioma [21-24]. Asbestos is no longer mined in the U.S. but is still imported [28]. Occupational and environmental exposure to asbestos fibers (amphibole forms such as crocidolite and amosite are most carcinogenic) remains a key risk factor globally. The latency period is typically decades.
risk_factors.strength: Strong for pleural mesothelioma; moderate for PeM. Population-based studies show a significant association in some regions [23].

risk_factors.factor: Germline mutations (BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome and other DNA repair gene variants)
risk_factors.detail: Germline mutations in BAP1, BRCA2, CDKN2A, TMEM127, VHL, WT1, MRE11A, and MSH6 are found in 12–16% of patients with mesothelioma (pleural and peritoneal) [48,72,73]. BAP1 germline mutations define a hereditary cancer syndrome (BAP1-TPDS) predisposing to mesothelioma, uveal melanoma, cholangiocarcinoma, and clear cell renal cell carcinoma [30]. Germline mutations appear more common in patients who are young, have a family history of mesothelioma, or have a history of other synchronous malignancies [48,72,74].
risk_factors.strength: Established; present in a clinically significant subset of patients (12-16%). Family history and young age are clues.

risk_factors.factor: Somatic ALK rearrangements and EWSR1::ATF1 fusions
risk_factors.detail: Rare peritoneal mesotheliomas in young adults (often ≤40 years) carry ALK rearrangements (e.g., STRN-ALK) or EWSR1::ATF1 fusions [49-51,54,70,71,101-103]. In one series of 25 young patients (≤40 years), 2 (8%) had an ALK rearrangement [102]. These patients typically lack asbestos exposure and predisposing germline mutations.
risk_factors.strength: Rare but clinically important due to targetability with ALK inhibitors (e.g., ceritinib, crizotinib) [53,54,104].

risk_factors.detail: Many patients with PeM have no identifiable asbestos exposure or known genetic predisposition; the disease may occur in younger individuals without typical risk factors [20].
risk_factors.strength: Common; indicates that other unknown environmental or genetic factors may be involved.

protective_factors: No protective factors are explicitly mentioned in the NCCN guideline for peritoneal mesothelioma.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Patients with peritoneal mesothelioma (PeM) typically present with abdominal signs and symptoms, often with a high symptom burden. The most common presenting features include ascites (77%), abdominal pain (69%), abdominal distension, and an abdominal mass (30%). Many patients also report weight loss, fatigue, anorexia, asthenia, nausea, early satiety, and intestinal obstruction [11,21,38]. Because these symptoms are nonspecific and the disease is rare, diagnosis is frequently delayed, and many patients have advanced disease at initial presentation [11,24,38]. The mean age at diagnosis is approximately 69 years [11]. PeM occurs in equal numbers of males and females, distinguishing it from pleural mesothelioma which is more common in males [11]. Although PeM can spread extensively within the abdominal cavity, it uncommonly metastasizes beyond the abdomen [24].

symptoms.name: Ascites
symptoms.detail: Fluid accumulation in the peritoneal cavity, often the most common presenting symptom.
symptoms.alarm: yes – rapid accumulation may indicate advanced disease

symptoms.name: Abdominal pain
symptoms.detail: Often diffuse or localized abdominal discomfort, can be dull or sharp.

symptoms.name: Abdominal distension
symptoms.detail: Swelling or bloating due to tumor mass or ascites.

symptoms.name: Abdominal mass
symptoms.detail: Palpable or detectable on imaging, often indicative of tumor burden.

symptoms.name: Weight loss
symptoms.detail: Unintentional weight loss, often associated with advanced disease.

symptoms.name: Fatigue
symptoms.detail: Persistent tiredness, common in cancer patients.

symptoms.name: Anorexia
symptoms.detail: Loss of appetite, contributing to weight loss.

symptoms.name: Nausea and early satiety
symptoms.detail: Feeling of fullness after small meals, may lead to reduced intake.

symptoms.name: Intestinal obstruction
symptoms.detail: Mechanical blockage due to tumor compression or infiltration.

signs.name: Abdominal distension
signs.detail: Visible swelling due to ascites or tumor masses.

signs.name: Palpable abdominal mass
signs.detail: Firm or fixed mass, often in the lower abdomen or pelvis.

signs.name: Ascites on examination
signs.detail: Shifting dullness or fluid wave indicating peritoneal fluid.

atypical_or_missed: PeM may be misdiagnosed as ovarian cancer due to similar peritoneal dissemination pattern [24].
May mimic tuberculous peritonitis, especially in regions with high tuberculosis prevalence [56].
Can present like alcoholic cirrhosis with ascites and abdominal distension [24].
Symptoms are nonspecific, leading to delayed diagnosis and advanced stage at presentation [11,24,38].
Less commonly, PeM presents with abnormal uterine bleeding (due to peritoneal irritation) [55].

red_flags: Rapid onset or refractory ascites requiring frequent paracentesis.
Unexplained bowel obstruction in the absence of prior surgery or hernia.
Persistent abdominal pain with associated weight loss or anorexia.
Palpable abdominal or pelvic mass in a patient without known malignancy.
Symptoms of peritoneal irritation (e.g., diffuse abdominal tenderness, guarding) without infectious cause.
Elevated soluble mesothelin-related peptide (SMRP) or CA-125 in the setting of peritoneal findings (optional markers) [21,24].
Family history of mesothelioma or BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome – consider genetic testing [29-37,48].

diagnostic.test: CT chest with contrast + CT or MRI abdomen/pelvis with contrast
diagnostic.rationale: Initial imaging to evaluate extent of peritoneal disease, assess for thoracic involvement, and identify bulky disease or visceral metastases.
diagnostic.key_findings: Diffuse peritoneal thickening, omental cake, ascites, nodules along peritoneal surfaces, absence of primary tumor elsewhere [41,42,43].

diagnostic.rationale: Allows direct visualization of peritoneal surfaces and targeted biopsy of nodules/masses. Also assesses resectability for complete cytoreduction [1,24].
diagnostic.key_findings: Multiple peritoneal nodules or masses; biopsy provides tissue for histologic subtype determination.

diagnostic.test: Image-guided core biopsy (if bulky disease/visceral organ metastasis)
diagnostic.rationale: Alternative when laparoscopy is not feasible or safe; obtains sufficient tissue for diagnosis and subtyping.
diagnostic.key_findings: Core tissue showing mesothelial proliferation with invasion.

diagnostic.rationale: Ki-67 labeling index by IHC is independently prognostic for OS after CRS+HIPEC; >9% is a high-risk feature [45].
diagnostic.key_findings: Percentage of proliferating tumor cells; >9% associated with worse survival.

diagnostic.rationale: Serum biomarker that may correlate with disease status; can be used as a serial marker for monitoring [21,24].
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated levels suggest mesothelioma; not diagnostic alone.

diagnostic.rationale: Screening for psychosocial distress including social determinants of health; recommended per guidelines [PEM-1 footnote a].
diagnostic.key_findings: Score indicating need for further evaluation.

staging.test: CT chest with contrast + CT or MRI abdomen/pelvis with contrast
staging.rationale: Used to determine extent of peritoneal disease and identify bicavitary (thoracic) involvement.
staging.indication: All patients at baseline and for surveillance after treatment.

staging.rationale: May help identify distant metastases and assess metabolic activity of peritoneal disease, especially when considering surgery.
staging.indication: Consider if there is suspicion of extra-abdominal spread or to characterize equivocal findings on CT/MRI.

staging.rationale: Gold standard for staging peritoneal disease burden; allows calculation of Peritoneal Cancer Index (PCI) and assessment of completeness of cytoreduction potential.
staging.indication: All patients being considered for CRS+HIPEC.

biomarkers.rationale: Loss of nuclear BAP1 expression (complete absence or cytoplasmic staining) is specific for mesothelioma and helps distinguish from reactive mesothelial proliferations [22,31-37].
biomarkers.when: At diagnosis on biopsy or resection specimen.
biomarkers.mandatory: Recommended for confirming malignancy, especially in equivocal cases.

biomarkers.rationale: Loss of cytoplasmic MTAP expression is a surrogate for CDKN2A homozygous deletion (9p21 loss); combined with BAP1 improves sensitivity for mesothelioma [35-37,39].
biomarkers.when: At diagnosis; particularly useful in pleural mesothelioma, but less sensitive in peritoneal (MTAP loss 14-16%) [40-44].
biomarkers.mandatory: Optional; can be used in conjunction with BAP1.

biomarkers.rationale: Detects homozygous deletion of 9p21, common in mesotheliomas; helps distinguish from reactive conditions [57-59].
biomarkers.when: When BAP1/MTAP IHC is inconclusive or for sarcomatoid histology.
biomarkers.mandatory: Optional; prevalence in peritoneal mesothelioma is 8-26% [40-44].

biomarkers.rationale: ALK rearrangements occur in rare peritoneal mesotheliomas (especially young patients) and predict dramatic response to ALK inhibitors (e.g., ceritinib) [49-54].
biomarkers.when: Consider in young patients (≤40 years) or those with no asbestos exposure.

biomarkers.rationale: Identifies rare driver alterations (e.g., NTRK fusions, ALK, EWSR1::ATF1) for targeted therapy and clinical trial options [27,28].
biomarkers.when: Recommended for all patients with peritoneal mesothelioma.
biomarkers.mandatory: Recommended per guidelines [PEM-D 1 of 3 footnote b].

do_not_miss: Midline laparoscopy with adequate biopsies for definitive histologic subtyping (epithelioid vs. biphasic vs. sarcomatoid) [1,24].
Immunohistochemistry panel: at least two mesothelial markers (calretinin, D2-40) and two carcinoma markers (claudin-4, TTF-1, CEA, PAX8) [17,26,58-64].
Ki-67 index if considering surgery, as >9% is a high-risk feature [45].
Assessment for germline BAP1 mutations in young patients or those with family history of mesothelioma [48,72].
Consider ALK testing in younger patients (especially children and young adults) [32,37,101-105].
avoid: Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of nodules/masses – cannot differentiate histologic subtypes [21,24].
Paracentesis fluid cytology alone – invasion cannot be assessed; cytology is inadequate for diagnosis [21,24].
Relying solely on imaging without tissue biopsy – imaging is not specific for PeM [41,42].

staging.system: No formal AJCC TNM staging system exists for peritoneal mesothelioma. The NCCN guidelines use the Peritoneal Cancer Index (PCI) and Completeness of Cytoreduction (CC) score for prognostic stratification and treatment planning. A novel TNM staging system based on the peritoneal carcinomatosis index has been proposed but is not universally adopted [81].

t_stages.description: There is no T category classification for peritoneal mesothelioma in the AJCC system. Disease burden is quantified using the Peritoneal Cancer Index (PCI), which divides the abdomen into 13 regions and assigns a score of 0 (no tumor) to 3 (tumor >5 cm) per region, yielding a total of 0-39 [PEM C 2 of 3].

n_stages.description: No formal N category. Lymph node metastasis is considered a high-risk feature [PEM-2 footnote e], but there is no standardized nodal staging. Nodal involvement is documented as present or absent.

m_stages.description: No M category. Distant metastasis beyond the abdomen is rare; bicavitary disease (thoracic involvement) is considered a high-risk feature [PEM-2 footnote e].

stage_groups.group: Low-risk features (medically operable, complete cytoreduction achievable)
stage_groups.criteria: Epithelioid histology, no high-risk features (no biphasic/sarcomatoid, no nodal metastasis, Ki-67 ≤9%, platelet count normal, PS 0-1, unicavitary disease, PCI ≤17, CC-0/CC-1 achievable) [PEM-2 footnotes c, e].
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Favorable prognosis; upfront CRS+HIPEC recommended. If no surgical/pathologic high-risk features, surveillance alone; if high-risk features present, consider adjuvant systemic therapy [PEM-2].
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: For epithelioid histology with complete cytoreduction (CC-0), 5-year survival approximately 47-50% [76,78]; median OS 53-63 months [76,77].

stage_groups.group: High-risk features (medically inoperable, incomplete cytoreduction, or presence of any high-risk feature)
stage_groups.criteria: Biphasic/sarcomatoid histology, nodal metastasis, Ki-67 >9%, thrombocytosis, PS=2, bicavitary disease, PCI >17, or CC >1 [PEM-2 footnote e].
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Poor prognosis; neoadjuvant systemic therapy is strongly encouraged, then re-evaluation for surgery [PEM C 1 of 3]. If still not operable, definitive systemic therapy ± supportive care.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: For biphasic with complete resection (CC-0) ~50% (median 6.8 years) but with incomplete resection much worse (median 2.8 years for CC-1, 4.3 months for CC-2) [78]. For sarcomatoid, survival is dismal.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Potentially curative if downstaged, otherwise palliative

stage_groups.criteria: Medically inoperable or complete cytoreduction not achievable, or PS 3-4 [PEM-2].
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Treat with systemic therapy (if PS 0-2) or best supportive care (if PS 3-4). Median OS with systemic therapy alone ~16-17 months for first-line pemetrexed/platinum [7,87].
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: ~20% overall for all PeM; cure rare [2,11,121-124].

staging_pearls: The Peritoneal Cancer Index (PCI) is the most commonly used intraoperative staging tool; scores >17 are considered high tumor burden and often preclude complete cytoreduction [PEM-2 footnote e].
Completeness of Cytoreduction (CC) score: CC-0 (no residual), CC-1 (<2.5 mm), CC-2 (2.5-25 mm), CC-3 (>25 mm) [PEM C 2 of 3]. CC-0 is goal; CC-1 may be acceptable for epithelioid subtype [2].
Bicavitary disease (thoracic + peritoneal) is a high-risk feature; systemic therapy is recommended before considering surgery [PEM C 1 of 3].
Histologic subtype is the single most important prognostic factor: epithelioid best, biphasic intermediate, sarcomatoid worst [15,54].
No AJCC TNM staging exists – PCI and CC scores serve as surrogate for staging.

management_principles.overview: The management of peritoneal mesothelioma (PeM) requires a multimodal approach integrating surgery and systemic therapy, with treatment decisions guided by histologic subtype, resectability, and performance status. PeM is a rare cancer accounting for approximately 15% of all mesotheliomas, with an estimated 300–400 cases in the United States annually [10]. One-year overall survival (OS) is approximately 46%, and 5-year OS is about 20% [11]. Cure is rare, but survival is improved for patients able to undergo complete cytoreductive surgery (CRS) with intraperitoneal chemotherapy [12–16]. All recommendations in the NCCN Guidelines are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with epithelioid histology, unicavitary disease, low-risk features (absence of high-risk features: biphasic/sarcomatoid histology, nodal metastasis, Ki-67 >9%, thrombocytosis, PS=2, bicavitary disease, high disease burden/incomplete cytoreduction [PCI >17, CC score >1]), and who are medically operable with complete cytoreduction achievable [PEM-2].
by_intent.approach: Upfront CRS + hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC). Postoperatively, if surgical/pathologic high-risk features are present, adjuvant systemic therapy is recommended; otherwise, imaging surveillance. Repeat CRS + HIPEC may be considered for recurrence >12 months from prior CRS in select patients [PEM-2].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients who are medically inoperable, have incomplete cytoreduction, have any high-risk features (biphasic/sarcomatoid histology, bicavitary disease, etc.), or have PS 3–4 (best supportive care) [PEM-2].
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy alone (chemotherapy and/or immunotherapy) as first-line treatment. For those who become medically operable after systemic therapy, consideration of CRS + HIPEC. Subsequent therapy upon progression.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with PS 0–2 who are not eligible for surgery or refuse surgery, including those with bicavitary disease, sarcomatoid/biphasic histology, or recurrence after prior CRS+HIPEC.
by_intent.approach: First-line systemic therapy based on histology: for epithelioid, preferred regimens include (carboplatin or cisplatin)/pemetrexed, with or without bevacizumab or pembrolizumab, or ipilimumab + nivolumab. For biphasic/sarcomatoid, preferred regimen is ipilimumab + nivolumab, with other options including (carboplatin or cisplatin)/pemetrexed. Subsequent therapy based on prior treatment [PEM-D].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Management by a multidisciplinary team with peritoneal mesothelioma experience is recommended [PEM-1]. The team should include surgical oncologists experienced in CRS and HIPEC, medical oncologists, diagnostic and interventional radiologists, pathologists with expertise in mesothelial lesions, and supportive care specialists. Decisions regarding surgical candidacy require careful evaluation of histology, extent of disease (PCI, bicavitary involvement), and performance status.
management_principles.performance_status_guide: For patients with PS 3–4, best supportive care is recommended (see Principles of Supportive Care and Survivorship). PS assessment is used to determine eligibility for surgery and systemic therapy. PS 0–2 are considered for active treatment; PS 3–4 are recommended for symptom management and palliative care [PEM-2].

management_pathways.title: Medically Operable with Complete Cytoreduction Achievable and Low-Risk Features
branching_basis: Low-risk features (epithelioid histology; absence of ANY high-risk features)

nodes.criteria: Patient is medically operable, complete cytoreduction achievable, and has low-risk features (epithelioid histology, no high-risk features).

options.label: Cytoreductive surgery (CRS) + hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC)
options.recommendation: Upfront CRS + HIPEC. Complete cytoreduction (CC-0) is the goal; CC-1 (<2.5 mm residual) is acceptable for epithelioid subtype [PEM-C].

adjuvant_logic.summary: After CRS+HIPEC, evaluate for presence of ANY surgical/pathologic high-risk features (biphasic/sarcomatoid histology, nodal metastasis, Ki-67 >9%, thrombocytosis, PS=2, bicavitary disease, high disease burden/incomplete cytoreduction [PCI >17, CC score >1]). If high-risk features present, then adjuvant systemic therapy is recommended. If no high-risk features, then imaging surveillance [PEM-2].

nodes.criteria: After CRS+HIPEC, surgical/pathologic high-risk features present.

options.label: Adjuvant systemic therapy (see systemic therapy pathways)
options.recommendation: Chemotherapy and/or immunotherapy based on histology and prior treatment. Regimens include (carboplatin or cisplatin)/pemetrexed with or without bevacizumab, ipilimumab + nivolumab, etc. [PEM-D]

nodes.criteria: After CRS+HIPEC, no surgical/pathologic high-risk features.

options.label: Imaging surveillance
options.recommendation: CT chest + CT or MRI abdomen/pelvis with contrast every 3–6 months x 5 years, then yearly [PEM-2].

management_pathways.title: Medically Inoperable, Incomplete Cytoreduction, or Presence of Any High-Risk Features
branching_basis: Presence of ANY high-risk features (biphasic/sarcomatoid histology, nodal metastasis, Ki-67 >9%, thrombocytosis, PS=2, bicavitary disease, high disease burden/incomplete cytoreduction [PCI >17, CC score >1])

nodes.criteria: Medically inoperable or complete cytoreduction not achievable, or presence of high-risk features (includes bicavitary disease, sarcomatoid/biphasic histology).

options.label: First-line systemic therapy (see histology-based pathways)
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy alone. For epithelioid: (carboplatin or cisplatin)/pemetrexed ± bevacizumab or pembrolizumab, or ipilimumab + nivolumab. For biphasic/sarcomatoid: ipilimumab + nivolumab (preferred) or (carboplatin or cisplatin)/pemetrexed ± bevacizumab [PEM-D].

nodes.criteria: After systemic therapy, patient becomes medically operable and complete cytoreduction achievable.

options.label: Consider CRS + HIPEC
options.recommendation: CRS+HIPEC may be considered if disease is downstaged and all criteria for surgery are met [PEM-2].

nodes.criteria: After systemic therapy, patient remains medically inoperable or complete cytoreduction not achievable.

options.label: Imaging surveillance
options.recommendation: CT chest + CT or MRI abdomen/pelvis with contrast every 3–6 months x 5 years, then yearly [PEM-2].

nodes.criteria: Progression after initial systemic therapy or surveillance.

options.label: Subsequent systemic therapy (see subsequent therapy pathways)
options.recommendation: If chemotherapy was first-line, preferred: nivolumab, ipilimumab + nivolumab. If ipilimumab + nivolumab was first-line, preferred: (carboplatin or cisplatin)/pemetrexed ± bevacizumab. Other recommended: pemetrexed, atezolizumab + bevacizumab (if no prior ICI), gemcitabine, vinorelbine [PEM-D].

management_pathways.title: First-Line Systemic Therapy for Epithelioid Histology (Non-Surgical Candidates)
branching_basis: Not eligible for CRS+HIPEC or refuses surgery

nodes.criteria: Epithelioid histology, PS 0–2, not surgical candidate

options.label: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Based on phase III data in pleural mesothelioma and expanded access programs [1-6].

options.label: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed + Bevacizumab
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Bevacizumab added to platinum/pemetrexed improved OS in MAPS trial [7-9].

options.label: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed + Pembrolizumab
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Pembrolizumab with chemotherapy showed improved PFS in phase 3 trial [10]. Subcutaneous pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph may be substituted.

options.label: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Based on CheckMate 743 in pleural mesothelioma, showing improved OS in non-epithelioid; extended to epithelioid per panel consensus [11].

options.label: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Gemcitabine
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Alternative for patients unable to receive pemetrexed [12-14].

options.label: Pemetrexed (single-agent)
options.recommendation: Category 2A. For patients not eligible for platinum [15].

options.label: Vinorelbine (single-agent)
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Based on MS01 trial [16].

management_pathways.title: First-Line Systemic Therapy for Biphasic or Sarcomatoid Histology
branching_basis: Not eligible for CRS+HIPEC or refuses surgery

nodes.criteria: Biphasic or sarcomatoid histology, PS 0–2, not surgical candidate

options.label: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Based on CheckMate 743 showing significant OS benefit in non-epithelioid histology [11].

options.label: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Standard platinum/pemetrexed chemotherapy [1-6].

options.label: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed + Bevacizumab
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Bevacizumab added to chemotherapy [7-9].

options.label: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Gemcitabine
options.recommendation: Category 2A [12-14].

options.label: Pemetrexed (single-agent)
options.recommendation: Category 2A [15].

options.label: Vinorelbine (single-agent)
options.recommendation: Category 2A [16].

management_pathways.title: Subsequent Systemic Therapy (After Progression on First-Line Treatment)
branching_basis: Prior first-line therapy type (chemotherapy vs immunotherapy)
Histology (epithelioid vs biphasic/sarcomatoid) – but recommendations are similar across histologies

nodes.criteria: Progression after first-line chemotherapy (platinum/pemetrexed-based) – regardless of histology

options.label: Nivolumab
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Based on CONFIRM trial (nivolumab vs placebo) in relapsed mesothelioma, median OS 10.2 vs 6.9 months, HR 0.69 [17,18].

options.label: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Based on MAPS2 and INITIATE trials [17,19].

options.label: Pemetrexed (single-agent)
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Consider rechallenge if good response to front-line pemetrexed-based treatment [20,21,29].

options.label: Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Only if no prior immune checkpoint inhibitor. Phase 2 trial in PeM showed ORR 40%, 1-year OS 85% [22].

options.label: Gemcitabine
options.recommendation: Category 2A [23,24].

options.label: Vinorelbine
options.recommendation: Category 2A [25,26].

nodes.criteria: Progression after first-line ipilimumab + nivolumab – regardless of histology

options.label: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed
options.recommendation: Category 2A [1-6].

options.label: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed + Bevacizumab
options.recommendation: Category 2A [7-9].

options.label: Pemetrexed (single-agent)
options.recommendation: Category 2A [20,21].

options.label: Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Only if no prior ICI, but patients have had ipilimumab+nivolumab so not applicable; listed for completeness [22].

options.label: Gemcitabine
options.recommendation: Category 2A [23,24].

options.label: Vinorelbine
options.recommendation: Category 2A [25,26].

management_pathways.title: Rare Driver Alterations: ALK Rearrangements
branching_basis: Identification of ALK rearrangement by molecular profiling (NGS, IHC)

nodes.criteria: ALK rearrangement identified in peritoneal mesothelioma (rare, primarily in young patients)

options.label: ALK inhibitor (e.g., ceritinib, crizotinib)
options.recommendation: Based on case reports showing dramatic responses. Ceritinib reported in a 13-year-old girl with STRN-ALK fusion [53,54,104]. Crizotinib reported in other cases [37,105]. Broad molecular tumor profiling is recommended to identify such rare drivers [27,28].

management_pathways.title: Peritoneal Inclusion Cyst and Well-Differentiated Papillary Mesothelial Tumor (WDPMT)
branching_basis: Diagnosis of peritoneal inclusion cyst or WDPMT (non-diffuse mesothelioma)

options.label: Imaging surveillance
options.recommendation: Observation with imaging surveillance [PEM-2, Discussion].

options.label: CRS with or without HIPEC
options.recommendation: CRS ± HIPEC may be considered [PEM-2, Discussion].

items.text: CT of chest with contrast + CT or MRI of abdomen/pelvis with contrast
items.recommendation: Essential for initial evaluation and staging. CT identifies diffuse peritoneal distribution, absence of lymph nodes or distant metastases suggests PeM [PEM-1, Discussion].
May be used to assess bicavitary disease and extent of disease [PEM-1].

items.text: Midline laparoscopy with biopsies of nodule/mass
items.recommendation: Recommended for diagnosis and to assess candidacy for complete cytoreduction. Provides adequate tissue for histologic typing (epithelioid, biphasic, sarcomatoid) [PEM-1].

items.text: Image-guided core biopsy if bulky disease/visceral organ metastasis
items.recommendation: Alternative when laparoscopy is not feasible [PEM-1].

Panel of at least two mesothelial markers (calretinin, D2-40) and two carcinoma markers (claudin-4, TTF-1, polyclonal CEA, PAX8) to confirm diagnosis. BAP1 IHC, MTAP IHC, CDKN2A FISH may aid in distinguishing from reactive proliferations [PEM-A].
Optional but may be useful for risk stratification; Ki-67 >9% is a high-risk feature associated with worse OS after CRS+HIPEC [PEM-1, 45].
Optional; levels may correlate with disease status [PEM-1].
Recommended to identify rare driver alterations (e.g., ALK rearrangements, NTRK fusions) for which effective drugs may be available, and to identify germline mutations (e.g., BAP1) [PEM-D, 27,28].

items.text: Assess for distress using NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, including social determinants of health
items.recommendation: New in version 2.2026; integrates distress screening into initial evaluation [PEM-1, a].
PS 0–2 candidates for active treatment; PS 3–4 recommended for best supportive care [PEM-2].

items.text: Assessment of low-risk vs high-risk features
items.recommendation: Low-risk: epithelioid histology, absence of any high-risk features. High-risk: biphasic/sarcomatoid histology, nodal metastasis, Ki-67 >9%, thrombocytosis, PS=2, bicavitary disease, high disease burden/incomplete cytoreduction (PCI >17, CC score >1) [PEM-2].

surgery.role: Surgery is a cornerstone of curative-intent treatment for peritoneal mesothelioma, specifically cytoreductive surgery (CRS) combined with hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC). It is recommended for carefully selected patients with medically operable disease and complete cytoreduction achievable, particularly those with epithelioid histology and low-risk features. Surgery is not recommended for patients with PS 3–4 or those with incomplete cytoreduction (unless palliative with minimal morbidity). The goal is macroscopic complete resection (CC-0) or near-complete (CC-1) for epithelioid subtype.
principles: All recommendations are from well-designed retrospective case-control or cohort studies [PEM-C].
Surgical resection should be performed by surgical oncologists with experience in managing peritoneal mesothelioma.
Decisions regarding surgical options are highly dependent on accurate histology; peritoneal biopsy must provide enough tissue for differentiation of subtypes. Cytology is generally not adequate.
For patients being considered for surgery, a laparoscopy is recommended to determine candidacy for complete cytoreduction [1].

The goal of surgery is complete gross cytoreduction: removal of ALL visible or palpable tumors (CC-0). CC-1 (<2.5 mm residual) is acceptable for epithelioid subtype, as a large multi-institutional study suggests <2% change in 5-year OS and unchanged median OS compared to CC-0 [2].
If complete cytoreduction is not possible, palliative surgery and/or HIPEC can be considered if minimal morbidity; otherwise surgery should be aborted [3].
Complete cytoreduction frequently requires total parietal peritonectomy, including visceral resections when necessary [4].

Resectable epithelioid mesothelioma should undergo upfront CRS+HIPEC. If no high-risk features (positive LN, incomplete cytoreduction, Ki-67 >9%), surveillance is sufficient. If high-risk features, adjuvant therapy recommended.
For biphasic, sarcomatoid, clinically positive LN, or high PCI >17, neoadjuvant therapy is strongly encouraged followed by reevaluation for complete CRS+HIPEC.
For bicavitary disease with minimal thoracic burden, systemic therapy is recommended; surgery can be considered in select cases [6].
If bevacizumab-containing regimen administered, at least 6-week interval between last dose and CRS [7,8].

Patients whose disease recurs in the peritoneum after CRS+HIPEC should be re-evaluated for repeat CRS+HIPEC; studies show this can be done safely with good outcomes in appropriately selected patients (>12 months from prior CRS) [13,14].

procedures.name: Cytoreductive Surgery (CRS)
procedures.approach: Total parietal peritonectomy with visceral resections as needed to achieve complete removal of all visible tumor. Completeness of cytoreduction scored as CC-0 (no residual), CC-1 (<2.5 mm), CC-2 (2.5–25 mm), CC-3 (>25 mm) [Sugarbaker 1999, 21].
procedures.indications: Medically operable patients with epithelioid histology, unicavitary disease, and complete cytoreduction achievable. Also considered in select biphasic/low-volume disease. Contraindicated in PS 3–4, sarcomatoid histology (usually), bicavitary disease (except select cases).
procedures.key_complications: Grade 3–4 complications in 31% of patients (multi-institutional study [76]), including acute kidney injury, hemorrhage, infection, anastomotic leak. Perioperative mortality reported in up to 3% in experienced centers [14,76,77].

procedures.name: Hyperthermic Intraperitoneal Chemotherapy (HIPEC)
procedures.approach: Delivered intraoperatively after cytoreduction. Perfusate heated to 42–43°C for 60–110 minutes. Regimens are typically cisplatin-based (cisplatin/doxorubicin, cisplatin alone, carboplatin, or cisplatin/mitomycin) [PEM-C].
procedures.indications: Performed at time of CRS for eligible patients. Use of platinum agents (cisplatin/carboplatin) associated with improved outcomes over mitomycin C in retrospective comparisons [9,10].
procedures.key_complications: Systemic toxicity (nephrotoxicity, neutropenia), hyperthermia-related complications, metabolic disturbances. Early postoperative or prolonged adjuvant IP therapy investigated but insufficient evidence for routine use outside clinical trials [11,12].

procedures.name: Palliative Surgery
procedures.approach: Limited resection or HIPEC considered only if minimal morbidity anticipated. Otherwise, surgery should be avoided.
procedures.indications: Symptomatic patients with incomplete cytoreduction possible but with high morbidity risk.
procedures.key_complications: Similar to CRS+HIPEC but risk-benefit must be carefully assessed.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Complete cytoreduction is defined as macroscopic complete resection (CC-0) or near-complete (CC-1) for epithelioid subtype. Resectability is determined by: 1) Medical operability (PS 0–2, adequate organ function). 2) Surgical feasibility: diffuse disease that can be completely removed (PCI ≤17 often cited as favorable; PCI >17 is high-risk). 3) Histology: epithelioid best candidates; biphasic/sarcomatoid often require neoadjuvant therapy. 4) Absence of bicavitary disease (except select low thoracic burden). 5) No evidence of extra-abdominal metastases. Laparoscopy is recommended for final decision [PEM-C].

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy is not recommended as a primary treatment modality for peritoneal mesothelioma. It can be used selectively for palliation of symptoms (e.g., pain control for localized metastases). No specific dose frameworks or concurrent chemotherapy regimens are provided in the NCCN guidelines for PeM.
principles: Radiation therapy is not part of the primary management algorithm for PeM.
May be used for palliation of symptomatic metastatic sites.
No high-level evidence supports routine use in PeM; recommendations are based on extrapolation from pleural mesothelioma where radiation is used for prophylaxis or palliation.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is indicated for patients with peritoneal mesothelioma who are not candidates for CRS+HIPEC, either due to medical inoperability, incomplete cytoreduction, high-risk features, or recurrent disease after surgery. Recommendations are largely extrapolated from clinical trials in pleural mesothelioma, with additional data from small PeM-specific studies. The NCCN Panel has preference-stratified regimens by histology (epithelioid vs biphasic/sarcomatoid) and line of therapy (first-line vs subsequent). All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [PEM-D]. Broad molecular tumor profiling is recommended to identify rare driver alterations (ALK, NTRK) [27,28].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed ± Bevacizumab or Pembrolizumab; Ipilimumab + Nivolumab
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on phase III trials in pleural mesothelioma (pemetrexed+cisplatin [1], MAPS trial adding bevacizumab [8], CheckMate 743 for ipilimumab+nivolumab [11], and phase III pembrolizumab+chemotherapy [10]). Expanded access programs confirm activity in PeM [2,3,7].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-line therapy – Biphasic or Sarcomatoid histology
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: CheckMate 743 demonstrated significant OS benefit in non-epithelioid histology (HR 0.46 for OS in sarcomatoid subgroup) [11].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed ± Bevacizumab [1-9]; (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Gemcitabine; Pemetrexed; Vinorelbine.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: CONFIRM trial showed OS benefit with nivolumab vs placebo (10.2 vs 6.9 months, HR 0.69) [18]. MAPS2 and INITIATE trials support dual ICI [17,19].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Pemetrexed (rechallenge if good prior response) [20,21,29]; Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab (if no prior ICI) [22]; Gemcitabine [23,24]; Vinorelbine [25,26].

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Standard chemotherapy backbone proven in pleural and peritoneal mesothelioma [1-9].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab (not applicable if prior ICI); Gemcitabine; Vinorelbine; Pemetrexed single-agent.

key_regimens.name: Pemetrexed + Cisplatin
key_regimens.cycle_days: Not specified in source; standard 21-day cycle per pleural mesothelioma protocols.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, fatigue, nausea, renal toxicity. Grade 3-4 neutropenia reported in 34.6% of PeM patients in EAP [7].

key_regimens.name: Pemetrexed + Carboplatin
key_regimens.setting: First-line (alternative when cisplatin not tolerated), subsequent

key_regimens.name: Pemetrexed + Cisplatin + Bevacizumab
key_regimens.setting: First-line (epithelioid, also other recommended for biphasic/sarcomatoid)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hypertension, bleeding, thromboembolism, proteinuria. Grade 3-4 adverse events include hypertension (40%) in atezolizumab/bevacizumab study [22].

key_regimens.name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab
key_regimens.setting: First-line (preferred for biphasic/sarcomatoid; also preferred for epithelioid), subsequent
key_regimens.cycle_days: CheckMate 743: nivolumab 360 mg q3w + ipilimumab 1 mg/kg q6w; or flat dosing per label. Nivolumab subcutaneous may be substituted for IV.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related adverse events: pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies, dermatitis. Grade 3-4 events in ~30%.

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab (single-agent)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related adverse events (lower rate than combination). Grade 3-4 events in 3% (diarrhea, infusion reaction) [18].

key_regimens.name: Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab
key_regimens.setting: Subsequent therapy (only if no prior ICI)
key_regimens.cycle_days: Per phase 2 trial [22]: atezolizumab 1200 mg q3w, bevacizumab 15 mg/kg q3w.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hypertension (40% grade 3), anemia, immune-related pancreatitis and thrombocytopenia (10% grade 3). ORR 40%, 1-year OS 85% [85].

key_regimens.name: IP Chemotherapy Regimens (HIPEC)

drugs.name: Cisplatin
drugs.dose: 50 mg/L or 50 mg/m² (with doxorubicin); 100–240 mg/m² alone

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Acute kidney injury (cisplatin), myelosuppression, systemic toxicity from absorption. Grade 3-4 complications in 31-39% of patients [76,77].

treatment_response_assessment.title: Treatment Response Assessment and Surveillance
treatment_response_assessment.timing: After completion of primary treatment (CRS+HIPEC with or without adjuvant therapy) or during systemic therapy, imaging surveillance is recommended as follows: CT chest + CT or MRI of abdomen/pelvis with contrast every 3–6 months for the first 5 years, then annually thereafter [PEM-2]. For patients on systemic therapy, response assessment is typically performed at 2–3 month intervals using the same imaging modalities.
response_logic: After CRS+HIPEC: If no surgical/pathologic high-risk features, proceed directly to surveillance. If high-risk features present, after adjuvant systemic therapy, then surveillance.
During systemic therapy: Imaging is used to assess response. Progression on imaging triggers change to subsequent systemic therapy.

For patients initially treated with systemic therapy (non-surgical candidates): imaging surveillance every 3–6 months; if progression, consider subsequent systemic therapy or, if patient becomes operable and complete cytoreduction achievable, consider CRS+HIPEC.
For patients with peritoneal inclusion cyst or WDPMT who are observed: imaging surveillance as clinically indicated.
imaging_recommendations: CT chest with contrast + CT or MRI of abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the standard modality for surveillance [PEM-2].
FDG-PET/CT may be considered for initial evaluation of bicavitary disease but is not routinely recommended for surveillance.
Imaging should be reviewed by radiologists familiar with peritoneal mesothelioma to assess disease burden (peritoneal cancer index) and detect new nodules or progression.
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: If progression is detected on imaging and the patient is a surgical candidate (>12 months from prior CRS) and otherwise operable, repeat CRS+HIPEC can be considered [PEM-2, j].
For patients not eligible for repeat surgery, subsequent systemic therapy should be initiated according to histology and prior treatment (see systemic therapy pathways).
Biopsy of new or enlarging lesions may be considered to confirm recurrence, especially if there is clinical ambiguity or suspicion of transformation to a different histology.
For WDPMT with microinvasive disease, CRS ± HIPEC may be considered if progression occurs.
In rare cases with ALK rearrangement, progression on targeted therapy may prompt rebiopsy for resistance mechanisms.

prognosis.overall: Peritoneal mesothelioma (PeM) is a rare malignancy with an estimated incidence of approximately 300–400 cases per year in the United States [Discussion-10]. The mean age at diagnosis is about 69 years [Discussion-11]. One-year overall survival (OS) is approximately 46%, and 5-year OS is about 20%; cure is rare [Discussion-11], [Discussion-2], [Discussion-121–124]. Survival is significantly improved for patients who undergo complete cytoreductive surgery (CRS) with intraperitoneal chemotherapy [Discussion-12–16]. Median OS for patients with epithelioid histology is 39 months versus 14 months for biphasic histology [Discussion-15]. In a multi-institutional study of CRS and hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC) in 401 patients, median OS was 53 months (range 1–235 months), with 3-year and 5-year survival rates of 60% and 47%, respectively [Discussion-76]. Another single-institution study reported median OS of 63.2 months (95% CI, 29.6–96.7) and 5-year survival of 42% [Discussion-77]. For patients with biphasic histology undergoing CRS+HIPEC, 5-year survival was 50.2% (median 6.8 years) with complete resection (CC-0) and 41.6% (median 2.8 years) with near-complete resection (CC-1); median survival was only 4.3 months with incomplete resection (CC-2) [Discussion-78].

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Approximately 47-50% (if undergoing CRS+HIPEC with complete cytoreduction)
by_stage.context: Based on multi-institutional and single-institution studies of CRS+HIPEC in patients with epithelioid peritoneal mesothelioma [Discussion-76], [Discussion-77], [Discussion-78].
From Peritoneal Surface Oncology Group International Registry; median survival for CC-2 is 4.3 months [Discussion-78].

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Approximately 20% overall 5-year survival for all patients with PeM [Discussion-11]
by_stage.context: Population-based data from SEER; includes all patients regardless of treatment [Discussion-11].

prognostic_factors: Histologic subtype: epithelioid best, biphasic intermediate, sarcomatoid worst [Discussion-15].
Ki-67 labeling index >9%: associated with worse OS after CRS+HIPEC (considered a high-risk feature) [PEM-A-45].
Peritoneal Cancer Index (PCI) >17: high disease burden, high-risk feature [PEM-2].
Completeness of cytoreduction (CC) score >1: residual disease >2.5 mm worsens survival [PEM-C-21].
Bicavitary disease (involvement of both peritoneum and pleura): high-risk feature [PEM-2].
BAP1 IHC loss and retained p16 expression: associated with prolonged survival in epithelioid pleural mesothelioma, data in PeM limited [PEM-A-46].
Germline BAP1 mutations: associated with 7-fold improved long-term survival in mesothelioma [PEM-A-47], [PEM-A-48].
prognosis.prognostic_context: Prognosis for peritoneal mesothelioma is highly dependent on histology, extent of disease, and ability to achieve complete cytoreduction. Patients with epithelioid histology, low PCI, and complete cytoreduction (CC-0) have the best outcomes, with median survival exceeding 5 years in select series. Biphasic and sarcomatoid histologies portend a worse prognosis, though complete resection can still yield prolonged survival. Inoperable patients have a median OS of approximately 1 year, and 5-year survival is low. The NCCN guidelines emphasize expert multidisciplinary evaluation and selection for CRS+HIPEC to optimize outcomes [Discussion-12], [Discussion-21]. The presence of high-risk features (KI-67 >9%, nodal metastases, incomplete cytoreduction) necessitates consideration of perioperative systemic therapy [PEM-2].

disease_related.management: Paracentesis or peritoneal catheter placement for symptomatic relief [PEM-B].
Pain management per NCCN Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain [PEM-B]; surgical debulking may be considered.
Supportive care including antiemetics (NCCN Guidelines for Antiemesis), nutritional support, and potentially palliative surgery [PEM-B].
Nutritional counseling, fatigue management per NCCN Guidelines for Cancer-Related Fatigue [PEM-B].

chemotherapy: Myelosuppression: neutropenia (grade 3-4 neutropenia 34.6% with pemetrexed + cisplatin), thrombocytopenia (3%), anemia (14%), leukopenia (21%) [Discussion-7], [Discussion-89].
Nausea/vomiting: managed with antiemetics per NCCN Antiemesis Guidelines [PEM-B].
Nephrotoxicity: acute kidney injury reported in 35.7% of patients after cisplatin-based HIPEC [Discussion-14].
Peripheral neuropathy: associated with platinum agents.
immunotherapy: Immune-related adverse events (irAEs): pancreatitis, thrombocytopenia, pneumonitis, myocarditis, endocrine disorders [PEM-D-1].
Management: refer to NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immunotherapy-Related Toxicities [PEM-B].
targeted_therapy: No specific targeted therapy toxicities detailed in the guideline; ALK inhibitors (ceritinib, crizotinib) used in rare ALK-rearranged cases may cause hepatotoxicity, fatigue, nausea [PEM-D-1].
surgery: Grade 3-4 complications occur in 26.2% to 38.9% of patients after CRS+HIPEC [Discussion-14], [Discussion-77].
Acute kidney injury: 35.7% in one series [Discussion-14].
Other: bleeding, infection, anastomotic leak, prolonged ileus.
radiation: Radiation therapy is not recommended as primary therapy for PeM but may be used selectively for palliation of symptomatic metastases (e.g., bone pain). Specific toxicity not detailed [Discussion-MS-5].

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care for patients with peritoneal mesothelioma is guided by NCCN symptom management and survivorship guidelines. Given the high symptom burden (ascites, pain, distension, fatigue), a multidisciplinary approach is essential. Recommendations include management of peritoneal effusions, pain, nausea/vomiting, psychosocial distress, fatigue, immunotherapy-related toxicities, venous thromboembolism, and palliative care. Smoking cessation and comprehensive survivorship care are also emphasized [PEM-B].
supportive_care.nutritional_support: The NCCN Guidelines do not provide a specific nutritional support protocol for PeM. However, given the frequent occurrence of early satiety, nausea, and weight loss, referral to a registered dietitian and use of nutritional supplements may be considered. For patients undergoing CRS+HIPEC, perioperative nutritional optimization is standard. The NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship and Palliative Care may offer guidance [PEM-B].
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Antiemetic prophylaxis is recommended for all patients receiving chemotherapy (e.g., pemetrexed/platinum, gemcitabine) and should follow the NCCN Guidelines for Antiemesis. For highly emetogenic regimens (e.g., platinum-based combinations), a three-drug regimen including a neurokinin-1 receptor antagonist, a 5-HT3 receptor antagonist, and a corticosteroid is standard. No specific PeM-specific antiemetic protocol is provided [PEM-B].
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: The NCCN Guidelines do not specifically address granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) for PeM. However, for patients receiving chemotherapy regimens associated with febrile neutropenia (e.g., pemetrexed/platinum), G-CSF prophylaxis may be appropriate. The NCCN Guidelines for Myeloid Growth Factors should be consulted [PEM-B].
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Venous thromboembolism (VTE) prophylaxis is recommended for hospitalized patients with cancer, including those with PeM. The NCCN Guidelines for Cancer-Associated Venous Thromboembolic Disease provide detailed recommendations. For patients undergoing CRS+HIPEC, perioperative VTE prophylaxis should be implemented [PEM-B].
supportive_care.pain_management: Pain management should follow the NCCN Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain. Given the frequent presentation with abdominal pain and distension, a comprehensive pain assessment is needed. Interventions may include analgesics (NSAIDs, opioids), interventional procedures (celiac plexus block, peritoneal catheter drainage), and palliative radiation for selected metastases [PEM-B].
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Psychosocial distress should be assessed using the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health [PEM-1]. Referral to psychosocial services, counseling, and support groups is recommended. The NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management provide guidance [PEM-B].
supportive_care.dental_care: No specific dental care recommendations are provided in the NCCN Guidelines for PeM. For patients receiving chemotherapy or immunotherapy, standard dental evaluation and management of mucositis and infections per general cancer care guidelines are advised.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: For patients who undergo complete cytoreduction (CRS+HIPEC) and have no high-risk features on pathology (e.g., epithelioid histology, negative lymph nodes, Ki-67 ≤9%, CC-0), imaging surveillance alone is recommended. If high-risk features are present (biphasic/sarcomatoid histology, nodal metastasis, Ki-67 >9%, thrombocytosis, PS=2, bicavitary disease, high disease burden/incomplete cytoreduction [PCI >17, CC>1]), adjuvant systemic therapy is recommended, followed by imaging surveillance [PEM-2]. The recommended surveillance schedule is CT chest + CT or MRI of abdomen/pelvis with contrast every 3–6 months for 5 years, then annually thereafter [PEM-2]. For patients who are medically inoperable or have incomplete cytoreduction, systemic therapy is first-line, followed by imaging surveillance [PEM-2]. Repeat CRS+HIPEC can be considered in patients who are >12 months from prior CRS and who are otherwise operable [PEM-2].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: The rationale for surveillance is early detection of recurrence, which may enable salvage therapy (repeat CRS+HIPEC, systemic therapy). Peritoneal mesothelioma has a high propensity for locoregional recurrence within the abdomen. Surveillance imaging allows identification of recurrent disease before the onset of debilitating symptoms, potentially improving the chance of successful repeat surgical intervention or effective systemic therapy [Discussion-21].
late_effects_screening: Chronic kidney disease: monitor renal function after cisplatin-based HIPEC; baseline and periodic renal assessment recommended [Discussion-14].
Peripheral neuropathy: assess for platinum-induced neurotoxicity [Discussion-14].
Bowel dysfunction: monitor for adhesions, obstruction, or chronic diarrhea after extensive peritonectomy [PEM-C].
Secondary malignancies: no specific screening recommended beyond general cancer survivorship guidelines.

Immunotherapy late effects: for patients treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors, monitor for endocrine dysfunction (thyroiditis, hypophysitis) and other chronic irAEs per NCCN Immunotherapy Toxicity Management [PEM-B].
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Recurrence in peritoneal mesothelioma is most commonly within the peritoneal cavity, often at sites of previous tumor involvement. Distant metastases are less common but can occur to pleura, lungs, or extra-abdominal sites. The pattern of recurrence influences treatment decisions: isolated peritoneal recurrence may be amenable to repeat CRS+HIPEC if the interval from initial surgery is >12 months and the patient remains a surgical candidate [PEM-2], [Discussion-13], [Discussion-14]. Systemic therapy is indicated for widespread or unresectable recurrence. Bicavitary recurrence (peritoneal + pleural) often carries a poor prognosis and is managed with systemic therapy [PEM-2].

clinical_follow_up_schedule: History and physical examination every 3–6 months for the first 5 years, then annually [PEM-2].
Assess for new abdominal symptoms, weight loss, fatigue, and signs of ascites or obstruction [PEM-1].
Monitor performance status and quality of life.
imaging_strategy: CT chest with contrast + CT or MRI of abdomen/pelvis with contrast every 3–6 months for 5 years, then yearly [PEM-2].
Consider FDG-PET/CT for initial staging and for evaluation of recurrence when CT findings are equivocal or for differentiating scar from tumor [PEM-1].
MRI is an alternative for abdominal/pelvic imaging, particularly if there is concern for peritoneal deposits not well seen on CT.
laboratory_monitoring: Soluble mesothelin-related peptide (SMRP) levels may be measured as an optional tumor marker; levels may correlate with disease status [PEM-1].

Complete blood count and comprehensive metabolic panel (including renal function) at each surveillance visit, especially if the patient has received platinum-based chemotherapy or has renal impairment [PEM-2].
No routine molecular marker monitoring is recommended.
supportive_follow_up: Psychosocial assessment using NCCN Distress Thermometer at each visit [PEM-1].
Referral to survivorship clinic per NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship [PEM-B].
Management of long-term toxicities: pain, fatigue, neuropathy, renal dysfunction [PEM-B].

special_situations.key_message: Surgery is typically contraindicated in patients with bicavitary disease, but may be considered in select patients with minimal pleural disease.
special_situations.management: Systemic therapy is recommended. For patients with minimal thoracic disease burden, surgery (CRS+HIPEC combined with pleurectomy/decortication) can be considered in select cases [PEM-C-6]. Neoadjuvant systemic therapy is strongly encouraged before reevaluation for surgery [PEM-C].
special_situations.evidence: Retrospective data suggest that pleurectomy/decortication with CRS+HIPEC may offer survival benefit for carefully selected patients [PEM-C-6].

special_situations.key_message: A rare but targetable driver alteration; molecular profiling should identify ALK rearrangements in young patients (<40 years) or those without asbestos exposure.
special_situations.management: Treatment with ALK inhibitors (e.g., ceritinib, crizotinib, brigatinib) has shown dramatic responses. Case reports describe complete pathological response after ceritinib [PEM-D-27], [PEM-D-28]. BRAF testing is recommended when ALK-negative [PEM-A-49–54].
special_situations.evidence: Case reports and small series: ~8% of young PeM patients harbor ALK rearrangements; response to ALK inhibitors documented [Discussion-32], [Discussion-37], [Discussion-102], [Discussion-104].

special_situations.key_message: Found in 12–16% of patients with pleural and peritoneal mesothelioma; associated with improved survival and increased risk of other malignancies.
special_situations.management: Consider genetic counseling and testing for patients with young age, family history of mesothelioma, or multiple synchronous malignancies [PEM-A-48], [PEM-A-72], [PEM-A-74]. Surveillance for other BAP1-associated cancers (uveal melanoma, cholangiocarcinoma, renal cell carcinoma) is recommended.
special_situations.evidence: Patients with germline BAP1 mutations have 7-fold improved long-term survival [PEM-A-47]. Germline mutations are more common in younger patients [PEM-A-48].

special_situations.situation: Well-differentiated papillary mesothelial tumor (WDPMT) and peritoneal inclusion cyst
special_situations.key_message: Benign or low-grade lesions that are managed differently from diffuse malignant mesothelioma.
special_situations.management: Asymptomatic and noninvasive WDPMT and peritoneal inclusion cyst: observation with imaging surveillance. Symptomatic, recurrent, or microinvasive disease: consider CRS with or without HIPEC [Discussion-MS-5].
special_situations.evidence: WDPMT is genetically distinct (TRAF7/CDC42 mutations) and carries excellent prognosis [PEM-A-76]. Peritoneal inclusion cyst (formerly ‘benign multicystic mesothelioma’) is benign [Discussion-47–49].

special_situations.key_message: A preinvasive lesion; diagnosis requires BAP1 loss by IHC or CDKN2A homozygous deletion (FISH or MTAP IHC).
special_situations.management: No mass lesions on imaging; surveillance is recommended. Multidisciplinary management advised [PEM-A-5].
special_situations.evidence: WHO classification 2021 introduced mesothelioma in situ; detection relies on ancillary markers [PEM-A-1], [PEM-A-4].

special_situations.key_message: PeM in women can mimic ovarian cancer; requires careful immunohistochemical panel (include PAX8, WT-1, calretinin, D2-40) for distinction.
special_situations.management: Same management principles as PeM overall, but ensure correct diagnosis to avoid inappropriate ovarian cancer therapy [PEM-A-65].
special_situations.evidence: PeM in females occurs equally to males, unlike pleural mesothelioma [Discussion-11]. PAX8 can be positive in some PeM, complicating differential [PEM-A-65].

special_situations.key_message: Best supportive care is recommended; aggressive therapy not indicated.
special_situations.management: Palliative care, symptom management (ascites drainage, pain control) per NCCN Palliative Care Guidelines [PEM-2], [PEM-B].
special_situations.evidence: Guideline recommendation based on poor outcomes with active treatment in this population [PEM-2].

clinical_pearls: 1. Complete cytoreduction (CC-0) is the goal of surgery; CC-1 (residual <2.5 mm) is acceptable for epithelioid subtype with no significant loss in survival [PEM-C-2].
2. Ki-67 index >9% is a high-risk feature that warrants consideration of adjuvant systemic therapy after CRS+HIPEC [PEM-2], [PEM-A-45].
3. In patients with biphasic or sarcomatoid histology, neoadjuvant systemic therapy is strongly encouraged before attempted CRS+HIPEC [PEM-C-1].
4. Ipilimumab + nivolumab is the preferred first-line regimen for biphasic/sarcomatoid histology and is a preferred option for epithelioid histology [PEM-D-1].
5. For patients who progress on checkpoint inhibitor therapy, switch to platinum/pemetrexed-based chemotherapy (with or without bevacizumab) [PEM-D-1].
6. ALK rearrangements are rare but actionable in young patients with PeM; broad molecular profiling is recommended [PEM-D-1].
7. Bevacizumab-containing regimens should be held for at least 6 weeks before CRS to minimize bleeding risk [PEM-C-7], [PEM-C-8].
8. Repeat CRS+HIPEC can be considered for recurrence >12 months after initial surgery, with careful patient selection [PEM-2], [PEM-C-13], [PEM-C-14].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Mesothelioma: Peritoneal (Version 2.2026)
guidelines_resources.notes: Comprehensive recommendations for diagnosis, staging, pathology, surgery, systemic therapy, supportive care, and survivorship. Updated annually.

guidelines_resources.organization: Peritoneal Surface Oncology Group International (PSOGI) / European Reference Network for Rare Adult Solid Cancers (EURACAN)
guidelines_resources.title: Peritoneal mesothelioma: PSOGI/EURACAN clinical practice guidelines for diagnosis, treatment and follow-up
guidelines_resources.notes: Published in European Journal of Surgical Oncology (2021). Provides consensus recommendations for diagnosis, surgical indications, HIPEC protocols, and follow-up [Discussion-21].

guidelines_resources.title: Treatment of Malignant Pleural Mesothelioma: ASCO Clinical Practice Guideline
guidelines_resources.notes: While primarily for pleural mesothelioma, many systemic therapy recommendations are extrapolated for peritoneal mesothelioma [Discussion-60].

guidelines_resources.title: The Chicago Consensus on Peritoneal Surface Malignancies: Management of Peritoneal Mesothelioma
guidelines_resources.notes: Published in Cancer (2020). Provides expert consensus on surgical and intraperitoneal chemotherapy regimens [PEM-C-15].

guidelines_resources.title: WHO Classification of Tumours: Thoracic Tumours (5th edition, 2021)
guidelines_resources.notes: Defines histologic types, introduces mesothelioma in situ, and updates terminology (malignant removed) [PEM-A-1].

guidelines_resources.title: Protocol for the examination of specimens from patients with malignant pleural mesothelioma (Version 4.1.0.0)
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides guidance on immunohistochemistry and reporting for mesothelioma; applicable to peritoneal cases [Discussion-58].

key_trials.full_name: First-line nivolumab plus ipilimumab versus chemotherapy in patients with unresectable malignant pleural mesothelioma
key_trials.population: Unresectable pleural mesothelioma (includes some peritoneal, but majority pleural)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Progression-free survival, objective response rate, safety.
key_trials.practice_change: Established nivolumab/ipilimumab as first-line standard for unresectable mesothelioma; extrapolated to peritoneal mesothelioma for biphasic/sarcomatoid and preferred for epithelioid [Discussion-107].

key_trials.full_name: Bevacizumab for newly diagnosed pleural mesothelioma in the Mesothelioma Avastin Cisplatin Pemetrexed Study (MAPS)
key_trials.practice_change: Added bevacizumab to cisplatin/pemetrexed as first-line option; extrapolated to peritoneal mesothelioma [Discussion-106].

key_trials.full_name: Nivolumab versus placebo in patients with relapsed malignant mesothelioma (CONFIRM)
key_trials.population: Pleural (95%) and peritoneal (5%) mesothelioma, relapsed after platinum chemotherapy
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Progression-free survival (HR 0.67), objective response rate (12% vs 0%).
key_trials.practice_change: Established nivolumab as second-line option; supports use of ICI in peritoneal mesothelioma after platinum progression [Discussion-88].

key_trials.full_name: Nivolumab or nivolumab plus ipilimumab in patients with relapsed malignant pleural mesothelioma
key_trials.population: Pleural mesothelioma, relapsed after first-line chemotherapy
key_trials.key_result: Disease control rate 44% (nivolumab) and 50% (nivolumab + ipilimumab). Median OS: 11.9 months (nivolumab) and 15.9 months (nivolumab + ipilimumab).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Objective response rate, progression-free survival, safety.
key_trials.practice_change: Showed activity of checkpoint inhibitors in relapsed mesothelioma; supports combination as subsequent therapy option [Discussion-113].

key_trials.full_name: Ipilimumab and nivolumab in the treatment of recurrent malignant pleural mesothelioma
key_trials.population: Pleural mesothelioma, recurrent after platinum-based chemotherapy
key_trials.intervention: Nivolumab 3 mg/kg every 2 weeks + ipilimumab 1 mg/kg every 6 weeks
key_trials.key_result: Objective response rate 38% (95% CI 23–55). Median PFS 6.2 months, median OS not reached at 12 months.
key_trials.practice_change: Contributed to evidence for ICI combination in recurrent mesothelioma [PEM-D-19].

key_trials.full_name: Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy versus chemotherapy in untreated advanced pleural mesothelioma
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS 7.2 months vs 5.5 months; HR 0.68 (95% CI 0.54–0.85; p=0.0008). Median OS data immature.
key_trials.practice_change: Added pembrolizumab to platinum/pemetrexed as first-line option; NCCN lists as preferred for epithelioid [PEM-D-10].

key_trials.full_name: A randomized phase III study of bevacizumab and standard chemotherapy with or without atezolizumab as first-line treatment for advanced pleural mesothelioma
key_trials.key_result: Median OS not reached vs 16.9 months; HR 0.72 (95% CI 0.58–0.90; p=0.004).
key_trials.practice_change: Demonstrated benefit of adding atezolizumab to bevacizumab-containing regimen; however, NCCN current version does not list this combination as first-line for peritoneal (only atezolizumab+bevacizumab as subsequent option) [PEM-D-9].

key_trials.full_name: Efficacy, safety, and biomarker analysis of combined PD-L1 (atezolizumab) and VEGF (bevacizumab) blockade in advanced malignant peritoneal mesothelioma
key_trials.population: Advanced unresectable peritoneal mesothelioma, progressive after platinum-based chemotherapy
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: PFS, duration of response, biomarker analysis.
key_trials.practice_change: Established atezolizumab + bevacizumab as a subsequent therapy option for patients not previously treated with ICIs [Discussion-85], [PEM-D-22].

key_trials.full_name: International Expanded Access Program using pemetrexed alone or in combination with a platinum agent for peritoneal mesothelioma
key_trials.population: Peritoneal mesothelioma not eligible for surgery
key_trials.key_result: 1-year OS 57.4% for pemetrexed+cisplatin; median OS 10.3 months for pemetrexed alone.
key_trials.practice_change: Provided primary evidence for pemetrexed/platinum in peritoneal mesothelioma [Discussion-7].
